title: Volvulus
oneLiner: Bowel twist causing closed-loop obstruction and ischaemia; sigmoid most common in elderly; prompt decompression needed to prevent perforation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Bowel ischaemia/gangrene**  
  - Single finding: **generalised peritonism** (rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds) + **raised lactate** (>2.5 mmol/L)  
  - Must differentiate from uncomplicated obstruction: ischaemic pain is constant, severe, out of proportion to examination  
- **Gastric volvulus** (rare, but rapidly fatal if missed)  
  - Single finding: **retching without vomiting** (Borchardt’s triad: epigastric pain, retching, inability to pass NG tube) + **shock**  
- **Perforation**  
  - Single finding: **free air under diaphragm** on erect CXR or AXR; patient toxic, rigid
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms**  
  - Sudden, severe colicky abdominal pain → becomes constant if ischaemic  
  - Abdominal distension (massive, tympanitic)  
  - Absolute constipation (no flatus or stool)  
  - Nausea/vomiting (late; early vomiting suggests gastric volvulus)  
- **Key exam findings**  
  - Distended, tympanic abdomen (“drum-like” percussion)  
  - Visible peristalsis (rare; late sign)  
  - Empty rectum on PR exam (pressing; indicates distal obstruction)  
  - High-pitched, absent or “metallic” bowel sounds  
  - Localised tenderness ± guarding if ischaemia
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sigmoid volvulus** vs **obstructive colonic carcinoma**  
  - **Splitter:** AXR shows “coffee-bean” sign with apex pointing to LUQ (sigmoid) vs. transition point with stricture (carcinoma)  
- **Caecal volvulus** (distension in RUQ, “coffee-bean” in RUQ on AXR, more likely in younger patients + prior abdominal surgery)  
- **Pseudodostruction (Ogilvie’s)** — no true obstruction; AXR shows dilated colon without transition point; often in elderly, immobile, polypharmacy  
- **Large bowel obstruction (benign stricture, diverticulitis)** — gradual onset, PR may show faecal impaction or mass  
- **Acute mesenteric ischaemia** — pain out of proportion, no distension initially, high lactate, atrial fibrillation
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour)**  
  - Vital signs + NEWS2  
  - Assess for peritonism  
  - Attempt NG tube (only in gastric volvulus with caution; if meets resistance, stop)  
  - **PR exam** — confirm empty rectum; check for faecal masses, blood, or mass  
- **Bloods**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP  
  - **Lactate** (critical marker of ischaemia)  
  - Group & screen or cross-match (if planning surgery)  
  - Consider VBG for quick lactate and base deficit  
- **Imaging**  
  - **First-line:** AXR (erect if possible) — look for:  
    - Sigmoid: “coffee-bean” sign, apex in LUQ; “epsilon” sign; dilated colon >6 cm  
    - Caecal: “coffee-bean” in RUQ, caecum >9 cm  
    - Free air → perforation (emergency)  
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (gold standard) — confirms volvulus, excludes other causes, assesses ischaemia (bowel wall thinning, pneumatosis, portal venous gas)  
  - **Gastric volvulus:** upright CXR + AXR — stomach above diaphragm; CT with oral contrast (if safe)  
- **Special tests**  
  - None routinely; consider water-soluble contrast enema if AXR equivocal (risk of perforation) — only after CT if safe  
- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Pregnancy test (urine or serum)** in all women of childbearing age before any radiation or surgery  
  - **Lactate** — if >4 mmol/L, suspect ischaemia or perforation
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - **NBM** immediately  
  - **IV access** (wide-bore, 14-16G)  
  - **Fluid resuscitation**: crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) — 500 mL–1000 mL bolus if shocked; reassess  
  - **Analgesia**: paracetamol 1 g IV if pain mild; morphine 2.5–5 mg IV (PRN) for moderate-severe (monitor BP, respiratory rate)  
  - **NG tube** if gastric volvulus suspected — but **do not force**; if no passage, stop and call senior  
  - **Urgent surgical review** — within 30 min if suspected ischaemia/perforation  
  - **Sigmoid volvulus (uncomplicated, no ischaemia)** : attempt decompression  
    - Rigid sigmoidoscopy (or flexible) by experienced clinician (usually surgical reg)  
    - Insert flatus tube (60-gauge rectal tube) 10–20 cm; confirm with passage of flatus/stool  
    - Secure tube to skin; leave for 24–48 h  
  - **If decompression fails** → may need water-soluble contrast enema under imaging or proceed to surgery  
  - **Caecal volvulus**: successful endoscopic decompression less common; often needs surgery (right hemicholectomy ± resection)  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h)**  
  - **If flatus tube placed**:  
    - Document daily flatus/stool output; monitor distension  
    - Keep tube on free drainage  
    - Repeat AXR after 24 h to confirm decompression  
    - Plan elective sigmoid colectomy (within same admission or next 2–4 weeks)  
  - **If surgery planned**:  
    - Optimise fluids and electrolytes, correct hypokalaemia  
    - Antibiotics if peritonitis or bowel preparation (local policy; often cefuroxime + metronidazole)  
    - Monitor for recurrence of volvulus  
  - **General care**  
    - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, unless bleeding risk or surgery imminent)  
    - Monitor NEWS2 4-hourly  
    - Encourage early mobilisation after decompression/surgery  
- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - **Elective surgery** recommended after first episode of sigmoid volvulus (recurrence risk ~50–70%)  
    - Sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis (open or laparoscopic)  
  - **High-risk patients** (elderly, frail, comorbidities) → consider percutaneous endoscopic colostomy (PEC) or conservative management with careful monitoring  
  - **Caecal volvulus** — resection (right hemicholectomy) is definitive; recurrence after decompression alone is high  
- **Default Orders** (if admitted for decompression or pre-surgery)  
  - **Fluids**: Hartmann’s 125–150 mL/h (adjust for AKI, cardiac status; [check local policy])  
  - **VTE prophylaxis**: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (omit day of surgery per local guidelines)  
  - **Diet**: NBM until flatus tube removed and obstruction resolved  
  - **Observation**: NEWS2 4-hourly; if tube in situ, monitor for leakage  
  - **Monitor**: stool/flatus chart, abdominal girth measurement daily
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Analgesia**  
  - Paracetamol 1 g IV/PO QDS (first line)  
  - Morphine sulfate 2.5–5 mg IV PRN severe pain (monitor respiratory rate, use naloxone if needed)  
- **Antibiotics** (if peritonitis or perforation)  
  - [check local policy] — typically co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS (or cefuroxime 750 mg IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS)  
- **Antiemetics** (if nausea/vomiting)  
  - Ondansetron 4–8 mg IV/IM/PO PRN (caution in prolonged QT)  
  - Cyclizine 50 mg IV/IM TDS PRN  
- **Key contraindications/interactions**  
  - Avoid NSAIDs (risk of GI bleeding, renal impairment)  
  - Avoid laxatives until obstruction resolved (may cause perforation)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**  
  - Morphine: avoid in severe renal impairment (accumulates); consider oxycodone 2.5–5 mg PO/IV PRN  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Giving opioids without monitoring respiratory depression** — always record NEWS and consider naloxone if RR <10  
  - **Prescribing antibiotics before confirming sensitivity/presence of peritonitis** — unnecessary in uncomplicated volvulus
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**  
  - **Strangulation/gangrene** (within hours) → perforation → peritonitis → septic shock  
  - **Recurrent volvulus** (especially sigmoid)  
  - **Perforation** during attempted decompression (rigid sigmoidoscopy)  
  - **Fluid and electrolyte imbalances** (hypokalaemia, dehydration)  
  - **Anastomotic leak** (if elective surgery)  
- **Chronic**  
  - **Recurrence** (if no definitive surgery)  
  - **Stricture** (following ischaemia without perforation)  
  - **Abdominal adhesions** after surgery
sections.Prognosis:
- **Uncomplicated** sigmoid volvulus → near 100% survival if decompressed promptly and elective resection performed  
- **Ischaemia/perforation** → mortality 20–50% (increases with age, delay in surgery)  
- **Recurrence** rate 50–70% after first episode if no surgery
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a PR exam** — an empty rectum in a massively distended patient is highly suggestive of volvulus  
- **Check AXR before calling surgical reg** — having the “coffee-bean” sign saves time and guides management  
- **Document successful decompression** clearly: “300 mL flatus and 100 mL liquid stool passed after tube insertion; abdomen less distended”  
- **If flatus tube fails or patient deteriorates** → urgent CT and surgical review (don’t repeat sigmoidoscopy)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT attempt NG tube in gastric volvulus without imaging** — can worsen situation or be impossible  
- **Do NOT leave flatus tube in place without a plan** — it must be removed within 48 h; if no improvement, escalate  
- **Do NOT give laxatives** (even PR) — risk of perforation in acute obstruction  
- **Do NOT delay surgery if signs of ischaemia** — waiting for “more tests” increases mortality
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Recurrent abdominal pain, distension, constipation (suggests recurrent volvulus)  
  - Fever, vomiting, inability to pass flatus  
  - Signs of peritonitis: severe pain, rigid abdomen, shortness of breath  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Surgical clinic within 2 weeks to discuss elective sigmoid colectomy (or PEC) after first episode  
  - If managed conservatively (e.g., elderly, frail), community team review weekly
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or rising  
  - New peritonism (guarding, rigidity)  
  - Decompression fails (no flatus/stool after 30 min)  
  - Hypotension (sBP <90 mmHg) not responding to fluids  
  - GCS <15 or new confusion  
- **ICU referral if:**  
  - Septic shock (pressor requirement)  
  - Respiratory failure  
  - Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2)

rawMarkdown:
## Volvulus

**One-liner:** Bowel twist causing closed-loop obstruction and ischaemia; sigmoid most common in elderly; prompt decompression needed to prevent perforation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Bowel ischaemia/gangrene**  
  - Single finding: **generalised peritonism** (rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds) + **raised lactate** (>2.5 mmol/L)  
  - Must differentiate from uncomplicated obstruction: ischaemic pain is constant, severe, out of proportion to examination  
- **Gastric volvulus** (rare, but rapidly fatal if missed)  
  - Single finding: **retching without vomiting** (Borchardt’s triad: epigastric pain, retching, inability to pass NG tube) + **shock**  
- **Perforation**  
  - Single finding: **free air under diaphragm** on erect CXR or AXR; patient toxic, rigid

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms**  
  - Sudden, severe colicky abdominal pain → becomes constant if ischaemic  
  - Abdominal distension (massive, tympanitic)  
  - Absolute constipation (no flatus or stool)  
  - Nausea/vomiting (late; early vomiting suggests gastric volvulus)  
- **Key exam findings**  
  - Distended, tympanic abdomen (“drum-like” percussion)  
  - Visible peristalsis (rare; late sign)  
  - Empty rectum on PR exam (pressing; indicates distal obstruction)  
  - High-pitched, absent or “metallic” bowel sounds  
  - Localised tenderness ± guarding if ischaemia  

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Sigmoid volvulus** vs **obstructive colonic carcinoma**  
  - **Splitter:** AXR shows “coffee-bean” sign with apex pointing to LUQ (sigmoid) vs. transition point with stricture (carcinoma)  
- **Caecal volvulus** (distension in RUQ, “coffee-bean” in RUQ on AXR, more likely in younger patients + prior abdominal surgery)  
- **Pseudodostruction (Ogilvie’s)** — no true obstruction; AXR shows dilated colon without transition point; often in elderly, immobile, polypharmacy  
- **Large bowel obstruction (benign stricture, diverticulitis)** — gradual onset, PR may show faecal impaction or mass  
- **Acute mesenteric ischaemia** — pain out of proportion, no distension initially, high lactate, atrial fibrillation  

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour)**  
  - Vital signs + NEWS2  
  - Assess for peritonism  
  - Attempt NG tube (only in gastric volvulus with caution; if meets resistance, stop)  
  - **PR exam** — confirm empty rectum; check for faecal masses, blood, or mass  
- **Bloods**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP  
  - **Lactate** (critical marker of ischaemia)  
  - Group & screen or cross-match (if planning surgery)  
  - Consider VBG for quick lactate and base deficit  
- **Imaging**  
  - **First-line:** AXR (erect if possible) — look for:  
    - Sigmoid: “coffee-bean” sign, apex in LUQ; “epsilon” sign; dilated colon >6 cm  
    - Caecal: “coffee-bean” in RUQ, caecum >9 cm  
    - Free air → perforation (emergency)  
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (gold standard) — confirms volvulus, excludes other causes, assesses ischaemia (bowel wall thinning, pneumatosis, portal venous gas)  
  - **Gastric volvulus:** upright CXR + AXR — stomach above diaphragm; CT with oral contrast (if safe)  
- **Special tests**  
  - None routinely; consider water-soluble contrast enema if AXR equivocal (risk of perforation) — only after CT if safe  
- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Pregnancy test (urine or serum)** in all women of childbearing age before any radiation or surgery  
  - **Lactate** — if >4 mmol/L, suspect ischaemia or perforation  

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - **NBM** immediately  
  - **IV access** (wide-bore, 14-16G)  
  - **Fluid resuscitation**: crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) — 500 mL–1000 mL bolus if shocked; reassess  
  - **Analgesia**: paracetamol 1 g IV if pain mild; morphine 2.5–5 mg IV (PRN) for moderate-severe (monitor BP, respiratory rate)  
  - **NG tube** if gastric volvulus suspected — but **do not force**; if no passage, stop and call senior  
  - **Urgent surgical review** — within 30 min if suspected ischaemia/perforation  
  - **Sigmoid volvulus (uncomplicated, no ischaemia)** : attempt decompression  
    - Rigid sigmoidoscopy (or flexible) by experienced clinician (usually surgical reg)  
    - Insert flatus tube (60-gauge rectal tube) 10–20 cm; confirm with passage of flatus/stool  
    - Secure tube to skin; leave for 24–48 h  
  - **If decompression fails** → may need water-soluble contrast enema under imaging or proceed to surgery  
  - **Caecal volvulus**: successful endoscopic decompression less common; often needs surgery (right hemicholectomy ± resection)  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h)**  
  - **If flatus tube placed**:  
    - Document daily flatus/stool output; monitor distension  
    - Keep tube on free drainage  
    - Repeat AXR after 24 h to confirm decompression  
    - Plan elective sigmoid colectomy (within same admission or next 2–4 weeks)  
  - **If surgery planned**:  
    - Optimise fluids and electrolytes, correct hypokalaemia  
    - Antibiotics if peritonitis or bowel preparation (local policy; often cefuroxime + metronidazole)  
    - Monitor for recurrence of volvulus  
  - **General care**  
    - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, unless bleeding risk or surgery imminent)  
    - Monitor NEWS2 4-hourly  
    - Encourage early mobilisation after decompression/surgery  
- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - **Elective surgery** recommended after first episode of sigmoid volvulus (recurrence risk ~50–70%)  
    - Sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis (open or laparoscopic)  
  - **High-risk patients** (elderly, frail, comorbidities) → consider percutaneous endoscopic colostomy (PEC) or conservative management with careful monitoring  
  - **Caecal volvulus** — resection (right hemicholectomy) is definitive; recurrence after decompression alone is high  
- **Default Orders** (if admitted for decompression or pre-surgery)  
  - **Fluids**: Hartmann’s 125–150 mL/h (adjust for AKI, cardiac status; [check local policy])  
  - **VTE prophylaxis**: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (omit day of surgery per local guidelines)  
  - **Diet**: NBM until flatus tube removed and obstruction resolved  
  - **Observation**: NEWS2 4-hourly; if tube in situ, monitor for leakage  
  - **Monitor**: stool/flatus chart, abdominal girth measurement daily  

### Drugs & Doses

- **Analgesia**  
  - Paracetamol 1 g IV/PO QDS (first line)  
  - Morphine sulfate 2.5–5 mg IV PRN severe pain (monitor respiratory rate, use naloxone if needed)  
- **Antibiotics** (if peritonitis or perforation)  
  - [check local policy] — typically co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS (or cefuroxime 750 mg IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS)  
- **Antiemetics** (if nausea/vomiting)  
  - Ondansetron 4–8 mg IV/IM/PO PRN (caution in prolonged QT)  
  - Cyclizine 50 mg IV/IM TDS PRN  
- **Key contraindications/interactions**  
  - Avoid NSAIDs (risk of GI bleeding, renal impairment)  
  - Avoid laxatives until obstruction resolved (may cause perforation)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment**  
  - Morphine: avoid in severe renal impairment (accumulates); consider oxycodone 2.5–5 mg PO/IV PRN  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Giving opioids without monitoring respiratory depression** — always record NEWS and consider naloxone if RR <10  
  - **Prescribing antibiotics before confirming sensitivity/presence of peritonitis** — unnecessary in uncomplicated volvulus  

### Complications

- **Acute**  
  - **Strangulation/gangrene** (within hours) → perforation → peritonitis → septic shock  
  - **Recurrent volvulus** (especially sigmoid)  
  - **Perforation** during attempted decompression (rigid sigmoidoscopy)  
  - **Fluid and electrolyte imbalances** (hypokalaemia, dehydration)  
  - **Anastomotic leak** (if elective surgery)  
- **Chronic**  
  - **Recurrence** (if no definitive surgery)  
  - **Stricture** (following ischaemia without perforation)  
  - **Abdominal adhesions** after surgery  

### Prognosis

- **Uncomplicated** sigmoid volvulus → near 100% survival if decompressed promptly and elective resection performed  
- **Ischaemia/perforation** → mortality 20–50% (increases with age, delay in surgery)  
- **Recurrence** rate 50–70% after first episode if no surgery  

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always perform a PR exam** — an empty rectum in a massively distended patient is highly suggestive of volvulus  
- **Check AXR before calling surgical reg** — having the “coffee-bean” sign saves time and guides management  
- **Document successful decompression** clearly: “300 mL flatus and 100 mL liquid stool passed after tube insertion; abdomen less distended”  
- **If flatus tube fails or patient deteriorates** → urgent CT and surgical review (don’t repeat sigmoidoscopy)  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do NOT attempt NG tube in gastric volvulus without imaging** — can worsen situation or be impossible  
- **Do NOT leave flatus tube in place without a plan** — it must be removed within 48 h; if no improvement, escalate  
- **Do NOT give laxatives** (even PR) — risk of perforation in acute obstruction  
- **Do NOT delay surgery if signs of ischaemia** — waiting for “more tests” increases mortality  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**  
  - Recurrent abdominal pain, distension, constipation (suggests recurrent volvulus)  
  - Fever, vomiting, inability to pass flatus  
  - Signs of peritonitis: severe pain, rigid abdomen, shortness of breath  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Surgical clinic within 2 weeks to discuss elective sigmoid colectomy (or PEC) after first episode  
  - If managed conservatively (e.g., elderly, frail), community team review weekly  

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or rising  
  - New peritonism (guarding, rigidity)  
  - Decompression fails (no flatus/stool after 30 min)  
  - Hypotension (sBP <90 mmHg) not responding to fluids  
  - GCS <15 or new confusion  
- **ICU referral if:**  
  - Septic shock (pressor requirement)  
  - Respiratory failure  
  - Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2)
